coredata.dc:title: Selective photoluminescence enhancement of red emitted surface modified poly(p-phenylenediamine) dots: An ultra-sensitive anion photoluminescence sensor for F− in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ye
Huang, Yiwen
Wang, Feiyu
Zhao, Jingjing
Zhang, Yiming
coredata.dc:description: The weak interactions between anions and common functional groups make it difficult for the development of anion photoluminescence sensors. In this work, we report an ultra-sensitive anion photoluminescence (PL) sensor for F− based on triethylene glycol monomethyl ether modified poly(p-phenylenediamine) dots (TPPDs). Due to the PET process in excited TPPDs, no obvious PL behavior can be observed in TPPDs. Due to the fast photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process in excited TPPDs, no obvious photoluminescence behavior can be observed: however, the PET process can be cut off in the presence of F− and TPPDs show bright red PL. The detection limit of this system is 2 × 10−10 M, which is much lower than most other reported detection methods. Compared with traditional “ON-OFF” PL sensors, such an “OFF-ON” PL detection system shows higher value in practical applications. The detection system exhibits superior photostability and high selectivity for F−. We also demonstrate the successful F− detection in vitro based on TPPDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Photoluminescence detection
Photoluminescence
Anion sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by grant from the Shanghai Pujiang Program (No. 16PJ1408400 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) size distribution histogram and (c) HR-TEM image of TPPDs.
(a) Schematic diagram showing the structure of TPPDs. (b) Raman spectrum, (c) XPS survey spectrum (d) N 1s and (e) O 1s XPS spectrum of TPPDs.
(a) Uv–vis spectrum and (b) Tauc plot of TPPDs. (c) Schematic diagram illustrating the detection mechanism of TPPDs.

(a) Photograph of 1.0 mg mL−1 TPPDs aqueous solution with 1 × 10-7 M F- under 365 nm UV light. (b) Normalized PLE and PL spectra of 1.0 mg mL−1 TPPDs aqueous solution with 1 × 10-7 M F-. (c) PL decay curve of 1.0 mg mL−1 TPPDs aqueous solution with 1 × 10-7 M F-. (d) PL spectra of TPPDs aqueous solution with different [F-] values ([F-] = 0–1 × 10-7 M, the concentration of TPPDs is 1.0 mg mL−1). (e) Linear relationship between PL intensity of TPPDs and [F-]. (f) The change of PL intensity of TPPDs aqueous solution with 1 × 10-7 M F- under Uv light irradiation (black curve) and visible light irradiation (red curve). (g) Influences of different ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cl-, Br-, OH-, SO4 2-, NO3- and NO2-) on the change in PL intensity of the mixture of TPPDs aqueous solution with 1 × 10-7 M F- at room temperature. F and F0 represent the PL intensity of TPPDs with and without the presence of different ions, respectively. The concentration of ions is 0.1 M. The concentration of TPPDs aqueous solution is 1.0 mg mL−1 (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Metabolic activity of rADSCs cells with different concentrations of TPPDs (0–600 μg mL−1). (b–c) Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of rADSCs (loaded with 100 μg/mL, TPPDs aqueous solution for 1 h) (b) without (c) with the presence of F- (1 × 10-7 M).

head.ce:title: Selective photoluminescence enhancement of red emitted surface modified poly(p-phenylenediamine) dots: An ultra-sensitive anion photoluminescence sensor for F− in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, Tongji University, 200072, Shanghai, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, Tongji University, 200072, Shanghai, PR China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Stomatology, Shanghai Tenth People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, Tongji University. No.301, Middle Yanchang Road, 200072, Shanghai, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ye Wang and Yiwen Huang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The weak interactions between anions and common functional groups make it difficult for the development of anion photoluminescence sensors. In this work, we report an ultra-sensitive anion photoluminescence (PL) sensor for F− based on triethylene glycol monomethyl ether modified poly(p-phenylenediamine) dots (TPPDs). Due to the PET process in excited TPPDs, no obvious PL behavior can be observed in TPPDs. Due to the fast photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process in excited TPPDs, no obvious photoluminescence behavior can be observed: however, the PET process can be cut off in the presence of F− and TPPDs show bright red PL. The detection limit of this system is 2 × 10−10 M, which is much lower than most other reported detection methods. Compared with traditional “ON-OFF” PL sensors, such an “OFF-ON” PL detection system shows higher value in practical applications. The detection system exhibits superior photostability and high selectivity for F−. We also demonstrate the successful F− detection in vitro based on TPPDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probe techniques have been widely used in fluorescence bio-imaging and detection since its development in last century [1–3]. Fluorescence detection has been demonstrated to have high application value in clinical medicine [4–6]. Based on different photoluminescence (PL) materials (such as fluorescent dyes, fluorescent proteins, semiconductor nanoparticles, carbon dots, graphene quantum dots and polymer dots), different PL sensors have been developed [4,7–11]. Ding et al. developed a series of PL sensors based on graphene quantum dots (such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, glutathione and hydroxyl radical) [4,12–14]. However, most reported PL sensors aimed to detect metal cations (mostly for heavy metal ions) [15]. This can be due to the high coordination activity of metal cations with functional groups (such as -SH, −OH and -NH2) [16]. In contrast, the weak interactions between anions and common functional groups make it difficult for the development of anion PL sensors [17]. F−, is a common ion that exists widely in diets of human [18,19]. Excess intake of F− is one of the main causes of musculoskeletal injury (such as skeletal fluorosis) [19–21]. Thus, the development of PL sensor for F− has practical significance.

Polymer dots are a kind of new PL materials that have attracted much attention recently due to low cost, high stability, good biocompatibility and controllable structure [22–26]. Liao et al. developed a PL sensor for NO2 − in vitro based on poly(o-phenylenediamine)-Rhodamine B copolymer dots [17]. The PL of poly(o-phenylenediamine)-Rhodamine B copolymer dots decreases as a result of fast photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the modulator (poly(o-phenylenediamine)) and the transducer, however, the N-NO2 bonding mode prevents PET, causing the enhanced fluorescence emission [17]. These results provide the structure design mentality for the development of anion PL sensors.

In this paper, we report triethylene glycol monomethyl ether modified poly(p-phenylenediamine) dots (TPPDs). Owing to the PET process in excited TPPDs, no obvious PL behavior is observed in TPPDs. However, the PET process can be cut off in the presence of F− and TPPDs show bright red PL. The detection limit of this system is 2 × 10-10 M that is significantly higher than most other reported detection methods. Compared with traditional “ON-OFF” PL sensors, such “OFF-ON” detection system shows higher value in practical applications. The detection system is shown to have superior photostability and high selectivity for F−. We also present the successful F− detection in vitro (rADSCs culture) based on TPPDs.

p-phenylenediamine, triethylene glycol monomethyl ether and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. The typical preparation process of TPPDs is as follows: 216 mg, 2 mmol p-phenylenediamine and 16.4 mg, 0.1 mmol triethylene glycol monomethyl ether were dissolved in 10.0 mL alcohol. The mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, we added 5 mL, 5 mM CaCl2 aqueous solution into the above reaction solution and the mixture was dialysed using a 3500 Da dialysis bag for one week. The yield of TPPDs is 7.2 wt.% (calculated from p-phenylenediamine and triethylene glycol monomethyl ether).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a Hitachi H-8100 (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption properties were characterized using a UV-5800 spectrophotometer (Yuanxi, China). PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected on a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used (PHI Quantera II system, INC, Japan).

The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows homogeneous TPPDs measuring 1–5 nm. A size distribution histogram of TPPDs is illustrated in Fig. 1b indicating the average size of the dots is 2.5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of the TPPDs (Fig. 1c) show a typical non-crystalline structure which indicates the obtained products are polymers.

The structure diagram of TPPDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. C–N bonds were formed between poly(p-phenylenediamine) and triethylene glycol monomethyl ether via alkylation reaction. Raman spectrometry was used to analyze the structure of TPPDs that had formed. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak located at 1613 cm−1 can be assigned to the benzene rings [26]. The peak located at 1431 and 1510 cm−1 can be attributed to NH bending deformation mode in TPPDs, respectively [26]. Moreover, the peaks at 1540 and 1590 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CC deformation of benzenoid rings and quinoid rings, respectively [26]. The XPS survey spectrum of TPPDs showed a C 1s peak at ca. 284.2 eV, an O 1s peak at ca. 532 eV, an N 1s peak at ca. 399 eV and a Ca 2p2/3 peak at ca. 346.8 eV (Fig. 2c) [27,28]. The well-fitted N 1s spectra of TPPDs are shown in Fig. 2d. The peaks located at 402.3, 400.3 ad 399.5 eV correspond to the signals of amino groups (-NH2), parahelium groups (C–NH–C) and aromatic N, respectively [29,30]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) of TPPDs can be divided into two different peaks. The peaks located at 533.4 eV and 532.4 eV correspond to the signals of COH/C-O-C and CO, respectively [31–33]. Moreover, the Ca2+ was combined with the triethylene glycol monomethyl ether groups by coordinate bonds [12].

The optical properties of TPPDs are shown in Fig. 3 . The Uv–vis spectrum of TPPDs (Fig. 3a) exhibited two absorption peaks at 275 and 380 nm. The peak at 275 nm is consistent with π-π* transition of the sp2 domains (aromatic nucleus) in TPPDs [34–36]. The peak located at 380 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition in TPPDs. The Uv–vis spectrum was fitted to the optical band gap by a Tauc plot [37]. It was worth noting that no obvious PL behavior can be observed in the TPPDs. This may be due to the PET process (Fig. 3b) in excited TPPDs [17]. However, the PET process can be cut off with the presence of F− (The concentration of F− ([F−]) is 1 × 10−7 M, Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 4 a, TPPDs appear bright red PL under the optimal excitation wavelength (621 nm). The emission wavelength of TPPDs is 749 nm and the corresponding φ is 0.76 (Fig. 4b): this value of φ is higher than that reported for most graphene quantum dots and carbon dots [37–63]. The PL lifetime (τ) of TPPDs is 4.2 ns (Fig. 4c) and the radiative rate (κr, κr = φ/τ) is 1.81 × 108 s-1. With the increased [F−] (0–1 × 10−7 M), the PL intensity of TPPDs was enhanced (Fig. 4d). The PL intensity of TPPDs shows good linear dependence with [F−] in the range of 0–3 × 10−8 M (Fig. 4e). The detection limit of this system is 2 × 10−10 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is much lower than most reported detection methods [64–68].

The TPPDs show superior photostability and high selectivity for F−. As shown in Fig. 4f and g, no obvious decrease in PL intensity of TPPDs with F− (1 × 10−7 M) was found under the long time UV light irradiation (Xe lamp with the center wavelength of 320 nm, 72 h) or visible light irradiation (450–850 nm, 7 days). Fig. 4h shows the PL intensity change of TPPDs + F− (The concentrations of TPPDs and F− are 100 μg mL-1 and 1 × 10−7, respectively) with the presence of different irons. No obvious change in PL intensity was observed in the presence of Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cl−, Br−, OH−, SO4 2-, NO3 − and NO2 −. This indicates high selectivity of this detection system.

TPPDs can be used in F− PL detection in vitro. For practical applications, the cytotoxicity of TPPDs was evaluated by rADSCs cells. No obvious reduction in cell viability of rADSCs cells (Fig. 5 a) was observed after TPPDs treatment at different concentrations (0–600 μg mL-1). The viability of rADSCs cells remained above 95% after 24 h TPPDs treatment (300 μg mL−1). These results indicate the TPPDs show low cytotoxicity in vitro which can be used in practical applications. Finally, we demonstrated the detection of F− in vitro with TPPDs. As shown in Fig. 5b, no obvious PL signal in rADSCs cells was observed after the introduction of TPPDs. However, bright red PL can be observed in the above system after the introduction of F− (NaF, 1 × 10−7 M). Compared with traditional “ON-OFF” PL sensors, the demonstrated “OFF-ON” detection system shows higher value in practical applications.

In summary, we developed an ultra-sensitive anion PL sensor for F− based on TPPDs. Due to the PET process in excited TPPDs, no obvious PL behavior was observed in TPPDs. However, the PET process can be cut off with the presence of F− and TPPDs show bright red PL. The detection limit of this system is 2 × 10-10 M which is much higher than most other reported detection methods. Compared with traditional “ON-OFF” PL sensors, such an “OFF-ON” detection system exhibits higher value in practical applications. The detection system shows superior photostability and high selectivity for F−. We also demonstrate successful F− detection in vitro based on TPPDs. This work is helpful for the development of anion PL sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by grant from the Shanghai Pujiang Program (No. 16PJ1408400).
